</thead>
<tbody>
<tr class="odd">
<td><span id="screenman_block_name_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Block Name</strong></td>
<td>(Required) This is the <strong>.01</strong> field of the BLOCK Multiple of the PAGE Multiple of the FORM (#.403) file. This field is a <strong>POINTER</strong> to the BLOCK (#.404) file.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><span id="screenman_block_order_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Block Order</strong></td>
<td>(Required) The <strong>Block Order</strong> determines the order in which users traverse fields on a page when they press <strong>&lt;PF1&gt;&lt;PF4&gt;</strong> to go to the next block or press the <strong>Enter</strong> key to move from the last field on one block to the first field on the next. When the user first reaches a page, ScreenMan places the user on the block with the lowest <strong>Block Order</strong> number.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong>Type of Block</strong></td>
<td><p>(Required) The <strong>Type of Block</strong> property can be either:</p>
<ul>
<li><p><strong>DISPLAY</strong>—DISPLAY blocks allow fields to be displayed but <em>not</em> changed by the user. Fields in a DISPLAY block are read-only.</p></li>
<li><p><strong>EDIT</strong>—EDIT blocks allow fields to be changed by the user. Adding an EDIT block to a form enables the editing of any <strong>DATA DICTIONARY</strong> fields placed on the EDIT-type block.</p></li>
</ul>
<p>When you first add a block to a form, you enter the properties for the block, including the type of block it is. You can also edit the properties of the block later.</p>
<p>![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/298.png) <strong>REF:</strong> For more information, see the <em>ScreenMan Tutorial for Developers Manual</em>, particularly Lessons 1-2 (DISPLAY blocks) and 1-7 (EDIT blocks). This manual is available on the <a href="http://www.va.gov/vdl/application.asp?appid=5">VDL</a>.</p></td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><span id="screenman_block_coordinate_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Block Coordinate</strong></td>
<td><p>(Required) This property defines the location of the block. The format of a coordinate is:</p>
<p>Row,Column</p>
<p>A <strong>Block Coordinate</strong> is defined relative to the page on which the block is defined. A Block Coordinate of “<strong>1,1</strong>”, for example, corresponds to the top left corner of the page. If a page is moved to a new position (i.e., if it is given a new coordinate), all blocks on the page move with it.</p></td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><span id="screenman_pointer_link_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Pointer Link</strong></td>
<td><p>This property is used if the fields displayed in this block are reached through a relational jump from the primary file of the form. The <strong>Pointer Link</strong> is a relational expression that describes this jump.</p>
<p>![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/299.png) <strong>REF:</strong> For more information, see the “Relational Navigation” section in the <em>VA FileMan Advanced User Manual</em>.</p></td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><strong>Pre Action<br />
<br />
</strong><strong>Post Action</strong></td>
<td><p>The <strong>Pre Action</strong> property is M code that is executed whenever the user reaches this block.</p>
<p>The <strong>Post Action</strong> property is M code that is executed whenever the user navigates away from this block.</p>
<p>Since these two properties are stored in the FORM (#.403) file, they apply to the block only as it is used on this form.</p></td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong><a href="#screenman_replication_property">Replication</a><br />
<br />
<a href="#screenman_index_property">Index</a><br />
<br />
<a href="#screenman_initial_position_property">Initial Position</a><br />
<br />
<a href="#screenman_disallow_laygo_property">Disallow LAYGO</a><br />
<br />
<a href="#screenman_field_for_selection_property">Field for Selection</a></strong></td>
<td><p>These properties pertain only to repeating blocks.</p>
<p>![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/300.png) <strong>REF:</strong> For more information about these properties, see the “<u>Displaying Multiples in Repeating Blocks</u>” section.</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<span id="_Toc204977301" class="anchor"></span>Table 89: ScreenMan Form Editor—Key Sequences to Move Screen Elements

#### Block Properties Stored in the BLOCK File

<u>Table 82</u> lists the Block properties stored in the BLOCK (#.404) file. Since these properties are stored in the BLOCK (#.404) file, they are part of the definition of the block itself. These properties apply to the block wherever it is used.

<table>
<caption><p><span id="_Toc204977302" class="anchor"></span>Table 90: ScreenMan Form Editor—Key Sequences to Add, Select, and Edit</p></caption>
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 29%" />
<col style="width: 70%" />
</colgroup>
<thead>
<tr class="header">
<th>Property</th>
<th>Description</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong>Name</strong></td>
<td>(Required) This is the <strong>.01</strong> field of the BLOCK(#.404) file. The block <strong>Name</strong> should be namespaced.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><span id="screenman_dd_number_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>DD Number</strong></td>
<td>This is the data dictionary (DD) number of the file or subfile that contains the fields that are placed on this block. A block can contain fields from only one file or subfile.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong>Disable Navigation</strong></td>
<td><p>If you set this property to <strong>YES</strong>, navigation within the block is disabled:</p>
<ul>
<li><p>Users <em>cannot</em> <strong>^</strong>-jump to other fields.</p></li>
<li><p>Users <em>cannot</em> <strong>^</strong>-jump to the command line.</p></li>
<li><p>The <strong>&lt;ARROW UP&gt;</strong>, <strong>&lt;ARROW DOWN&gt;</strong>, <strong>Tab</strong>, and <strong>PF4</strong> keys traverse the fields in the same order when pressing the <strong>Enter</strong> key (i.e., in the order established by the <a href="#screenman_field_order_property"><strong>Field Order</strong></a> property of the fields).</p></li>
<li><p>Users <em>cannot</em> use the <strong>&lt;PF1&gt;S</strong>, <strong>&lt;PF1&gt;E</strong>, <strong>&lt;PF1&gt;Q</strong>, and <strong>&lt;PF1&gt;C</strong> key sequences.</p></li>
</ul>
<p>If you set this property to <strong>OUTOK</strong>, navigation within the block is disabled, but the user can:</p>
<ul>
<li><p><strong>^</strong>-jump to the command line.</p></li>
<li><p>Use the <strong>&lt;PF1&gt;S</strong>, <strong>&lt;PF1&gt;E</strong>, <strong>&lt;PF1&gt;Q</strong>, and <strong>&lt;PF1&gt;C</strong> key sequences.</p></li>
</ul></td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><strong>Pre Action<br />
<br />
</strong><strong>Post Action</strong></td>
<td><p>The <strong>Pre Action</strong> property is M code that is executed whenever the user reaches this block.</p>
<p>The <strong>Post Action</strong> property is M code that is executed whenever the user navigates away from this block.</p>
<p>Since these two properties are stored in the BLOCK (#.404) file, they apply to the block as it is used on any page of any form.</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<span id="_Toc204977302" class="anchor"></span>Table 90: ScreenMan Form Editor—Key Sequences to Add, Select, and Edit

### Field Properties

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

<u>Table 83</u> lists the Field properties:

<table>
<caption><p><span id="_Toc204977303" class="anchor"></span>Table 91: ScreenMan Form Editor—General Key Sequences to: Exit, Quit, Save, and Obtain Help</p></caption>
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 29%" />
<col style="width: 70%" />
</colgroup>
<thead>
<tr class="header">
<th>Property</th>
<th>Description</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr class="odd">
<td><span id="screenman_field_type_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Field Type</strong></td>
<td><p>(Required) Four different types of fields can be defined on a block:</p>
<ul>
<li><p><span id="screenman_field_type_caption_only" class="anchor"></span><strong>Caption only—</strong>Used to display text on the screen. They have no data portion associated with them.</p></li>
<li><p><span id="screenman_field_type_dd" class="anchor"></span><strong>DATA DICTIONARY—</strong>Correspond to fields in a file. They have the following:</p></li>
</ul>
<ul>
<li><p>A data portion, which is the value of the field.</p></li>
<li><p>An optional caption portion, which serves to identify the data on the screen for the user.</p></li>
</ul>
<ul>
<li><p><span id="screenman_field_type_form_only" class="anchor"></span><strong>Form-only—</strong>Defined only on the form and are <em>not</em> tied to a field in a VA FileMan file.</p></li>
</ul>
<p>![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/301.png) <strong>REF:</strong> For more information about this field type, see the “<u>Form-only Fields</u>” section.</p>
<ul>
<li><p><span id="screenman_field_type_computed" class="anchor"></span><strong>COMPUTED—</strong>Like <strong>Form-only</strong> fields, <strong>COMPUTED</strong> fields are defined only on the form. You <em>cannot</em> place <strong>COMPUTED</strong> fields from VA FileMan files on a form. The computed expression is defined on the form and can be based on other fields on the form. Users <em>cannot</em> navigate to <strong>COMPUTED</strong> fields.</p></li>
</ul></td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><span id="screenman_field_order_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Field Order</strong></td>
<td>(Required) The <strong>Field Order</strong> number determines the order in which users traverse the fields in the block as they press the <strong>Enter</strong> key. <strong>Field Order</strong> is the <strong>.01</strong> field of the Field Multiple of the BLOCK (#.404) file.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong>Field</strong></td>
<td>(Required for <strong>DATA DICTIONARY</strong>-type fields.) The <strong>Field</strong> property applies only to <a href="#screenman_field_type_dd"><strong>DATA DICTIONARY</strong></a>-type fields. It identifies a field in a VA FileMan file or subfile. The <a href="#screenman_dd_number_property"><strong>DD Number</strong></a> of the block identifies the file or subfile that contains the field.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><span id="screenman_unique_name_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Unique Name</strong></td>
<td>You can optionally give fields on a block a <strong>Unique Name</strong>. A <strong>Unique Name</strong> is <em>never</em> seen by the user. They can be used to identify fields in some of the ScreenMan utilities, such as <a href="#putddsval-stuff-data-into-a-data-dictionary-field"><strong>PUT^DDSVAL</strong></a> and <a href="#getddsval-retrieve-data-from-a-data-dictionary-field"><strong>$$GET^DDSVAL</strong></a>, and in the computed expressions of <strong>COMPUTED</strong> fields. No two fields on a block can have the same <strong>Unique Name</strong>.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><span id="screenman_caption_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Caption<br />
<br />
</strong><strong>Executable Caption<br />
<br />
</strong><strong>Suppress Colon After Caption</strong></td>
<td><p>A <strong>Caption</strong> is uneditable text that appears on the screen. Captions of <a href="#screenman_field_type_dd"><strong>DATA DICTIONARY</strong></a>, <a href="#screenman_field_type_form_only"><strong>Form-only</strong></a>, and <a href="#screenman_field_type_computed"><strong>COMPUTED</strong></a> fields serve to identify for the user the data portion of the fields. Captions of these types of fields are automatically followed by a colon, unless the <strong>Suppress Colon After Caption</strong> property is set to <strong>YES</strong>. Captions of <a href="#screenman_field_type_caption_only"><strong>Caption-only</strong></a> fields have no associated data element and are <em>not</em> automatically followed by a colon.</p>
<p>If you want the text of a caption to be determined whenever the page is painted, you can enter M code as an <strong>Executable Caption</strong>. The code should set the local variable <strong>Y</strong> equal to the text you want displayed. A field with an <strong>Executable Caption</strong> <em>must</em> have <strong>!M</strong> as a <strong>Caption</strong>.</p></td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><strong>Default<br />
<br />
</strong><strong>Executable Default</strong></td>
<td><p>You can assign a Default to a <a href="#screenman_field_type_dd"><strong>DATA DICTIONARY</strong></a> or <a href="#screenman_field_type_form_only"><strong>Form-only</strong></a> type field on a form. ScreenMan presents the <strong>Default</strong> value to the user if the value of the field is <strong>NULL</strong> when the page on which the field is located is first displayed. Since ScreenMan validates the <strong>Default</strong>, it <em>must</em> be valid, unambiguous, and in external form; otherwise, it is <em>not</em> used.</p>
<p>If the field is a Multiple field, you can assign one of the defaults listed in <u>Table 84</u>:</p>
<table>
<caption><p><span id="_Ref343064302" class="anchor"></span>Table 92: ScreenMan Form Editor—Navigating: Cursor Movement and Keyboard Combination</p></caption>
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 34%" />
<col style="width: 65%" />
</colgroup>
<thead>
<tr class="header">
<th>Default</th>
<th>Subrecord Displayed</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr class="odd">
<td>FIRST</td>
<td>The subrecord with the lowest IEN.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td>LAST</td>
<td>The subrecord with the highest IEN.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td>Subrecord number</td>
<td>The subrecord with the specified IEN.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>Here, the characters “<strong>FIRST</strong>” and “<strong>LAST</strong>” are keywords that ScreenMan interprets as the subrecords with the lowest and highest IENs, respectively.</p>
<p>If the value of the default should be determined at the time the page is first presented to the user, you can enter M code as an <strong>Executable Default</strong>. The code should set the local variable <strong>Y</strong> equal to a valid, unambiguous value in external form. If the default in <strong>Y</strong> is invalid, it is <em>not</em> used. A field with an <strong>Executable Default</strong> <em>must</em> have <strong>!M</strong> as a <strong>Default</strong>.</p></td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><span id="screenman_data_length_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Data Length</strong></td>
<td><p>(Required for all field types, except <a href="#screenman_field_type_caption_only"><strong>Caption-only</strong></a>.) <strong>Data Length</strong> defines the length of the edit window for the data portion of fields. Ideally, the <strong>Data Length</strong> should equal the maximum length of the external form of the data (i.e., the form displayed to the user).</p>
<p>The <strong>Data Length</strong> of a <strong>WORD-PROCESSING</strong> field need only be <strong>1</strong>, since the contents of the field are <em>not</em> displayed in the edit window. A <strong>Data Length</strong> of <strong>1</strong> gives the cursor a place to rest when the user navigates to the field. When the user presses the <strong>Enter</strong> key at the field, control is passed to the user’s Preferred Editor, where the text can be displayed and, if allowed, edited.</p>
<p>If you define a <strong>Data Length</strong> smaller than a field’s maximum size, ScreenMan still provides two ways for the user to see the entire value of the field:</p>
<ul>
<li><p>Since the edit window is a “scrolling window,” text outside the confines of the edit window can scroll in, as text in the window scrolls out.</p></li>
<li><p>When the cursor is within an edit window, the user can press <strong>&lt;PF1&gt; Z</strong> to invoke the “zoom editor.” An area opens in the Command Area where up to <strong>245 characters</strong> can be seen and edited at once.</p></li>
</ul>
<blockquote>
<p>![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/302.png) <strong>NOTE:</strong> You <em>cannot</em> define an edit window that wraps around to a second line. In addition, the edit window <em>must not</em> extend into the rightmost column of the screen. This space <em>must</em> be left blank, so that the cursor has a place to rest beyond the last character of the data value. You <em>must</em>, therefore, never define a <strong>Data Length</strong> that causes the edit window to extend beyond the next to last column of the screen (i.e., column <strong>79</strong> on an <strong>80</strong>-column display).</p>
</blockquote></td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><strong>Caption<br />
<br />
</strong><strong>Data Coordinates</strong></td>
<td><p>(Required if a field has a caption or data portion.) <strong>Caption</strong> and <strong>Data Coordinates</strong> define the location of fields on the screen and are relative to the coordinate of the block on which they are defined. The format of coordinate is:</p>
<p><strong>Row,Column</strong></p>
<p>The coordinate “<strong>1,1</strong>” for example, corresponds to the block’s top left corner (i.e., the first column on the first row of the block).</p>
<p>The Form Editor simplifies the process of assigning coordinates to captions and data. It allows you to drag and drop fields on the screen, and thus, eliminates the need to explicitly assign values to the coordinate properties.</p>
<p>![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/303.png) <strong>REF:</strong> The Form Editor is described in detail in the “<u>ScreenMan Form Editor</u>” section.</p></td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong>Right Justify</strong></td>
<td>Set the <strong>Right Justify</strong> property to <strong>YES</strong> to display the data for the field to be right-justified.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><span id="screenman_required_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Required</strong></td>
<td><p>You can make any <em>non</em>-Multiple field on a form required. Making a field required on a form does <em>not</em> affect the definition of the field in the data dictionary. You need <em>not</em> make a field required that is already required by its data definition. The captions of required fields are underlined for easy identification.</p>
<p>![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/304.png) <strong>REF:</strong> For more information on how ScreenMan checks <strong>Required</strong> fields before filing, see the “<u>Data Filing (When Is It Performed?)</u>” section.</p>
<p>Before filing, ScreenMan checks that:</p>
<ul>
<li><p><strong>Required</strong> fields on all pages that can be accessed via the <a href="#screenman_next_page_property"><strong>Next Page</strong></a> and <a href="#screenman_previous_page_property"><strong>Previous Page</strong></a> links have values, even if you have <em>not</em> accessed those pages during the editing session.</p></li>
<li><p>If you have accessed any subpages, required fields in those subpages <em>must</em> also have values.</p></li>
</ul>
<p>If any required field is empty, the user <em>cannot</em> file any data changes. When they attempt to file, ScreenMan displays a list of those fields that require values.</p>
<p>![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/305.png) <strong>NOTE:</strong> “Popup” pages <em>not</em> accessed during the editing session are <em>not</em> checked for <strong>Required</strong> fields.</p>
<p>You can change the <strong>Required</strong> property on-the-fly while a form is running by making a call to <a href="#reqddsutl-change-required-property-of-a-field-on-a-form"><strong>REQ^DDSUTL</strong></a>.</p></td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong>Display Group</strong></td>
<td><p><strong>Display Group</strong> helps users resolve ambiguity when they attempt to <strong>^</strong>-jump to a field that has a caption that is <em>not</em> unique. If more than one field has the same caption, when users try to <strong>^</strong>-jump to a field with that caption, they are presented with a list of fields from which to choose. The text in the <strong>Display Group</strong> property is displayed in parentheses after the caption to help the user identify the correct field.</p>
<p>For example, if two fields have the caption “NAME:”, but one of those fields has a <strong>Display Group</strong> “Next of Kin,” when users enter <strong>^NAME</strong>, they are asked to choose between “NAME” and “NAME (Next of Kin)”.</p></td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><span id="screenman_disable_editing_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Disable Editing<br />
<br />
</strong><strong>Disallow LAYGO</strong></td>
<td><p>If you set the <strong>Disable Editing</strong> property to <strong>YES</strong>, users <em>cannot</em> navigate to the field, unless the field is a <strong>WORD-PROCESSING</strong> field. If the field is defined as uneditable in the data dictionary (DD), users <em>cannot</em> navigate to it—even if the field’s value is <strong>NULL</strong>.</p>
<p>![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/306.png) <strong>REF:</strong> For information about uneditable <strong>WORD-PROCESSING</strong> fields, see the “Word-processing Fields” section in the <em>VA FileMan User Manual</em>.</p>
<p>If you set the <strong>Disable Editing</strong> property to “<strong>REACHABLE</strong>,” users can navigate to the field, but they <em>cannot</em> change the value. For example, you might want to make an uneditable field reachable if you want to attach <a href="#screenman_branching_logic_property"><strong>Branching Logic</strong></a> to that field, to take the user to another page when they press the <strong>Enter</strong> key. You might also want to make an uneditable field reachable if the data value <em>cannot</em> fit in an edit window. Then the user can navigate to the field and see the entire contents of the field, either by scrolling the data in the edit window or by invoking the “zoom editor.”</p>
<p>You can change the <strong>Disable Editing</strong> property on-the-fly while a form is running by making a call to <a href="#unedddsutl-change-disable-editing-property-of-a-field-on-a-form"><strong>UNED^DDSUTL</strong></a>.</p>
<p>![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/307.png) <strong>NOTE:</strong> Fields on display blocks are always uneditable. On display blocks, users can navigate only to Multiple and <strong>WORD-PROCESSING</strong> fields.</p>
<p>Disabling editing for Multiple fields has no meaning. However, you can prevent users from adding new entries into a Multiple by setting the <strong>Disallow LAYGO</strong> property to <strong>YES</strong>. Multiple fields on display blocks automatically prohibit <strong>LAYGO</strong>.</p></td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><span id="screenman_data_validation_field_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Data Validation</strong></td>
<td>ScreenMan uses the definition of a field to automatically validate values input by the user. You can use the <strong>Data Validation</strong> property to validate the value even further. <strong>Data Validation</strong> is M code that is executed after the user enters a new value for a field and after the automatic validation that ScreenMan normally performs. If the code sets the <strong>DDSERROR</strong> variable, ScreenMan rejects the value. You might also want to ring the bell and make a call to <a href="#hlpddsutl-print-help-messages-in-the-command-area"><strong>HLP^DDSUTL</strong></a> to display a message to the user that indicates the reason the value was rejected.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><span id="screenman_subpage_link_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Subpage Link</strong></td>
<td>A subpage can be linked to a parent page by the <strong>Subpage Link</strong> property. The <strong>Subpage Link</strong> <em>must</em> be equal to the <a href="#screenman_page_number_property"><strong>Page Number</strong></a> of the subpage.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><span id="screenman_branching_logic_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Branching Logic<br />
<br />
</strong><span id="screenman_pre_action_field_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Pre Action<br />
<br />
</strong><span id="screenman_post_action_field_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Post Action<br />
<br />
</strong><span id="screenman_post_action_on_chg_property" class="anchor"></span><strong>Post Action on Change</strong></td>
<td><p>These properties contain M code that is executed at the times listed in <u>Table 85</u>:</p>
<table>
<caption><p><span id="_Ref494376963" class="anchor"></span>Table 93: ScreenMan Form Editor—Changing Current Page: Shortcut Keys</p></caption>
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 37%" />
<col style="width: 62%" />
</colgroup>
<thead>
<tr class="header">
<th>Property</th>
<th>Executed</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong>Branching Logic</strong></td>
<td>When the user presses the <strong>Enter</strong> at the field.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><strong>Pre Action</strong></td>
<td>Right before the user lands on the field.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong>Post Action</strong></td>
<td>When the user leaves the field.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><strong>Post Action on Change</strong></td>
<td>When the user leaves the field, and only if the user changed the value of the field.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>The code in the <strong>Branching Logic</strong>, <strong>Pre Action</strong>, <strong>Post Action</strong>, <strong>Post Action on Change</strong>, and <strong>Data Validation</strong> at the field level can rely on the variables listed in <u>Table 86</u>:</p>
<table>
<caption><p><span id="_Ref389650437" class="anchor"></span>Table 94: ScreenMan Form Editor—General Key Sequences to: Move Screen Elements</p></caption>
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 28%" />
<col style="width: 71%" />
</colgroup>
<thead>
<tr class="header">
<th>Local Variable</th>
<th>Description</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong>X</strong></td>
<td>The current internal value of the field.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td><strong>DDSEXT</strong></td>
<td>The current external value of the field.</td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td><strong>DDSOLD</strong></td>
<td>The previous internal value of the field.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>The <strong>Post Action</strong> and <strong>Post Action on Change</strong> are <em>not</em> executed when the user:</p>
<ul>
<li><p>Times out at a field.</p></li>
<li><p>Enters a caret (<strong>^</strong>) to go to the Command Area.</p></li>
<li><p>Enters a <strong>^</strong>-jumps to another field.</p></li>
</ul></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<span id="_Toc204977303" class="anchor"></span>Table 91: ScreenMan Form Editor—General Key Sequences to: Exit, Quit, Save, and Obtain Help

## ScreenMan Menu Options

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

The ScreenMan \[DDS SCREEN MENU\] menu, which is locked with the XUSCREENMAN security key, is found on a submenu of the Other Options \[DIOTHER\] menu, as shown in <u>Figure 229</u>:

<span id="_Ref494361397" class="anchor"></span>Figure 229: ScreenMan Forms—ScreenMan Menu Options

Select VA FileMan Option: <span class="mark">OTHER OPTIONS \<Enter\></span>

Select Other Options Option: <span class="mark">SCREENMAN \<Enter\></span>

Select ScreenMan Option: <span class="mark">? \<Enter\></span>

Answer with SCREENMAN OPTION NUMBER, or NAME

Choose from:

1 [EDIT/CREATE A FORM](#editcreate-a-form)

2 [RUN A FORM](#run-a-form)

3 [DELETE A FORM](#delete-a-form)

4 [PURGE UNUSED BLOCKS](#purge-unused-blocks)

Select SCREENMAN OPTION:

The following sections describe these four ScreenMan options.

### Edit/Create a Form

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

Use the Edit/Create a Form \[DDS EDIT/CREATE A FORM\] option on the ScreenMan \[DDS SCREEN MENU\] menu to invoke the Form Editor, the screen-oriented utility for editing and building ScreenMan forms.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/308.png) REF: The Form Editor is described in detail in the “<u>ScreenMan Form Editor</u>” section.

### Run a Form

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

Instead of setting up input variables and making a call to [^DDS](#dds-invoke-screenman), you can use the Run A Form \[DDS RUN A FORM\]option on the ScreenMan \[DDS SCREEN MENU\] menu to run a form, as shown in <u>Figure 230</u>:

<span id="_Ref494355096" class="anchor"></span>Figure 230: ScreenMan Forms—Run a Form Option

Select SCREENMAN OPTION: <span class="mark">RUN A FORM \<Enter\></span>

MODIFY SCREEN TEMPLATE FOR WHAT FILE: <span class="mark">ZZEZ SCREENDOC \<Enter\></span>

Select FORM: <span class="mark">ZZEZ DOC \<Enter\></span> ZZEZ DOC

Enter number of first page: 1// <span class="mark">\<Enter\></span>

Select ZZEZ SCREENDOC NAME: <span class="mark">FMUSER,ONE \<Enter\></span> M. FM

You are asked to select a

- File
- Form
- Initial page
- Record

The Run A Form \[DDS RUN A FORM\] option *cannot* run a form used to edit a subfile directly.

### Delete a Form

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

You can use the Delete A Form \[DDS DELETE A FORM\] option on the ScreenMan \[DDS SCREEN MENU\] menu to delete a form from the FORM (#.403) file, and any or all blocks used on that form from the BLOCK (#.404) file.

<span id="_Toc204977016" class="anchor"></span>Figure 231: ScreenMan Forms—Delete a Form Option

Select SCREENMAN OPTION: <span class="mark">DELETE A FORM \<Enter\></span>

MODIFY SCREEN TEMPLATE FOR WHAT FILE: <span class="mark">ZZEZ SCREENDOC \<Enter\></span>

Select FORM to delete: <span class="mark">ZZTEST DOC \<Enter\></span> ZZTEST DOC

\#55 02/16/91 User \#14 File \#16500

Once you have selected a file and form to delete, a short report is printed that lists all blocks used on the form, as illustrated in <u>Figure 232</u>:

<span id="_Ref342291856" class="anchor"></span>Figure 232: ScreenMan Forms—Delete a Form Option: Report of All Blocks Used on the Form

BLOCKS USED ON FORM "ZZTEST DOC" (IEN \#55)

Internal Used on

Entry Number Block Name Other Forms? Deletable?

------------ ---------- ------------ ----------

178 ZZTEST DOC HDR1 NO YES

179 ZZTEST DOC1 NO YES

180 ZZTEST DOC2 NO YES

181 ZZTEST DOC3 NO YES

182 ZZTEST DOC HDR3 NO YES

Delete a Form Data (<u>Figure 232</u>):

- The “Entry Number” column lists the internal entry numbers of the blocks used on the form.
- the “Block Name” column lists the names of the blocks.
- The “Used on Other Forms?” column indicates whether the blocks are used on other forms.
- The “Deletable?” indicates whether you can delete those blocks from the BLOCK (#.404) file. Only those blocks that are *not* used on other forms can be deleted.

You are then asked whether you want to delete the blocks used on the form from the BLOCK (#.404) file, as shown in <u>Figure 233</u>:

<span id="_Ref494355499" class="anchor"></span>Figure 233: ScreenMan Forms—Delete a Form Option: Delete Blocks

Delete all deletable blocks used on form ZZTEST DOC

from the BLOCK file (Y/N)? YES// <span class="mark">? \<Enter\></span>

Enter 'Y' to delete blocks used on form

ZZTEST DOC from the BLOCK file.

(Only blocks not used on other forms can be deleted.)

Enter 'N' to delete the form but not the blocks.

Delete all deletable blocks used on form ZZTEST DOC

from the BLOCK file (Y/N)? YES// <span class="mark">\<Enter\></span>

If you answer NO, the form is deleted from the FORM (#.403) file, but none of the blocks used on the form are deleted. If you choose *not* to delete a block, and that block is *not* used on any form, the only way to delete the block is to run the [Purge Unused Blocks](#purge-unused-blocks) \[DDS PURGE UNUSED BLOCKS\] option.

If you answer YES, you are asked whether you want to delete those blocks *without* confirmation.

<span id="_Toc204977019" class="anchor"></span>Figure 234: ScreenMan Forms—Delete a Form Option: Delete Blocks with or without Confirmation

Delete blocks without prompting (Y/N)? NO// <span class="mark">? \<Enter\></span>

Enter 'Y' to delete blocks from the BLOCK file

without confirmation.

Enter 'N' to confirm each delete.

Delete blocks without prompting (Y/N)? NO//

If you answer YES, all blocks used on the form that are *not* used on any form are deleted.

If you answer NO, you are prompted before any block is deleted. This gives you a chance to delete only specific blocks.

<span id="_Toc204977020" class="anchor"></span>Figure 235: ScreenMan Forms—Delete a Form Option: Deleting Blocks without Confirmation

Continue (Y/N)? NO// <span class="mark">YES \<Enter\></span>

Deleting form ZZTEST DOC (IEN \#55) ...

ZZTEST DOC HDR1 Delete (Y/N)? NO// YES

ZZTEST DOC1 Delete (Y/N)? NO// YES

ZZTEST DOC2 Delete (Y/N)? NO// YES

ZZTEST DOC3 Delete (Y/N)? NO// YES

ZZTEST DOC HDR3 Delete (Y/N)? NO// YES

DONE!

### Purge Unused Blocks

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

You can use the Purge Unused Blocks \[DDS PURGE UNUSED BLOCKS\] option on the ScreenMan \[DDS SCREEN MENU\] menu to delete any or all unused blocks from the BLOCK (#.404) file that are associated with a specific file.

<span id="_Toc204977021" class="anchor"></span>Figure 236: ScreenMan Forms—Purge Unused Blocks Option

Select OPTION: <span class="mark">OTHER OPTIONS \<Enter\></span>

Select OTHER OPTION: <span class="mark">SCREENMAN \<Enter\></span>

PURGE UNUSED BLOCKS FROM WHAT FILE: <span class="mark">ZZEZ SCREENDOC \<Enter\></span>

Once you have selected a file, a short report is printed that lists the blocks that are *not* used on any forms:

<span id="_Toc204977022" class="anchor"></span>Figure 237: ScreenMan Forms—Purge Unused Blocks Option: Report of Unused Blocks on any Forms

UNUSED BLOCKS ASSOCIATED WITH FILE ZZEZ SCREENDOC (#16500)

Entry Number Block Name

------------ ----------

You are then asked whether to delete the blocks without confirmation:

<span id="_Toc204977023" class="anchor"></span>Figure 238: ScreenMan Forms—Purge Unused Blocks Option: Delete Blocks with or without Confirmation

If you answer YES, all unused blocks are deleted.

If you answer NO, you are prompted before any block is deleted. This gives you a chance to delete only specific blocks.

<span id="_Toc204977024" class="anchor"></span>Figure 239: ScreenMan Forms—Purge Unused Blocks Option: Delete Blocks without Confirmation

DONE!

## Callable Routines

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

ScreenMan provides several callable routines. Many of these routines can be called from the various form properties that execute M code. These callable routines are described in the “<u>ScreenMan API</u>” section.

## Programmer Mode Utilities

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

### ^DDGF: Invoke the Form Editor from Programmer Mode

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

Use the ^DDGF API to invoke the Form Editor from Programmer mode.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/309.png) NOTE: You can also reach the Form Editor through the VA FileMan \[DIUSER\] menu options. On the VA FileMan \[DIUSER\] menu, select Other Options \[DIOTHER\], ScreenMan \[DDS SCREEN MENU\], and Edit/Create a Form \[DDS EDIT/CREATE A FORM\].

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/310.png) REF: The Form Editor is described in detail in the “<u>ScreenMan Form Editor</u>” section.

### CLONE^DDS: Make a Copy of a Form

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

Use the CLONE^DDS API to make a copy of a form. All blocks used on the form are copied and a new form that uses the new blocks is created.

In <u>Figure 240</u>, CLONE^DDS is used to make a copy of the XUEDIT CHARACTERISTICS form of the NEW PERSON (#200) file:

<span id="_Ref389650185" class="anchor"></span>Figure 240: ScreenMan Forms—CLONE^DDS: Sample Dialog to Copy a Form

\><span class="mark">D CLONE^DDS \<Enter\></span>

CLONE FORM FROM WHAT FILE: <span class="mark">NEW PERSON \<Enter\></span>

Select FORM to clone: <span class="mark">??</span>

Choose from:

XUEDIT CHARACTERISTICS \#1 12/06/90 File \#200

XUEXISTING USER \#2 12/12/90 File \#200

Select FORM to clone: <span class="mark">XUEDIT CHARACTERISTICS \<Enter\></span> XUEDIT CHARACTERISTICS

\#1 12/06/90 File \#200

Once you have selected a form to clone, a report that lists the blocks used on the form is printed, as shown in <u>Figure 241</u>:

<span id="_Ref494357309" class="anchor"></span>Figure 241: ScreenMan Forms—CLONE^DDS: Report Showing Blocks Used on a Form

BLOCKS USED ON FORM "XUEDIT CHARACTERISTICS" (IEN \#1)

Internal

Entry Number Block Name

------------ ----------

1 XUEDIT CHARACTERISTICS HDR

2 XUEDIT CHARACTERISTICS

Enter RETURN to continue or '^' to exit: <span class="mark">\<Enter\></span>

You *must* assign names to the new form and blocks you are creating. If the original form and blocks are namespaced (i.e., start with the same set of characters), you can choose to give the new form and blocks the same name, but with the namespace replaced with another set of characters. Then, when you are asked to enter new names, names that have the namespace replaced with the set of characters are displayed as defaults, as shown in <u>Figure 242</u>:

<span id="_Ref494357399" class="anchor"></span>Figure 242: ScreenMan Forms—CLONE^DD: Assigning New Form and Block Names

The new form and blocks must be given unique names.

Give the new form and blocks the same names as the original,

but a different namespace? YES// <span class="mark">\<Enter\></span>

Original namespace: <span class="mark">XU \<Enter\></span>

New namespace: <span class="mark">ZZ \<Enter\></span>

Enter names for the new form and blocks.

Original form name: <span class="mark">XUEDIT CHARACTERISTICS \<Enter\></span>

New form name: <span class="mark">ZZEDIT CHARACTERISTICS \<Enter\></span>

Original block name: <span class="mark">XUEDIT CHARACTERISTICS HDR \<Enter\></span>

New block name: <span class="mark">ZZEDIT CHARACTERISTICS HDR \<Enter\></span>

Original block name: <span class="mark">XUEDIT CHARACTERISTICS \<Enter\></span>

New block name: <span class="mark">ZZEDIT CHARACTERISTICS \<Enter\></span>

After you have given names to the new form and blocks, you are ready to clone the form as shown in <u>Figure 243</u>:

<span id="_Ref494357441" class="anchor"></span>Figure 243: ScreenMan Forms—CLONE^DDS: Cloning a Form

Ready to clone form? <span class="mark">YES \<Enter\></span>

Creating new blocks ...

ZZEDIT CHARACTERISTICS HDR \#71

ZZEDIT CHARACTERISTICS \#72

Creating new form ...

ZZEDIT CHARACTERISTICS \#36

Repointing to new blocks ...

Reindexing new form ...

DONE!

\>

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/311.png) NOTE: Be sure to check the properties of the cloned form and blocks for namespaced variables, block references, etc., that need to be modified manually.

### PRINT^DDS: Print a Form

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

Use the PRINT^DDS API to print a form. PRINT^DDS prints the properties of the form and the properties of all the blocks used on that form, as shown in <u>Figure 244</u>:

<span id="_Ref494357846" class="anchor"></span>Figure 244: ScreenMan Forms—PRINT^DDS: Printing a Form

### RESET^DDS: Reset Terminal, Variables, and Remove Temp Data

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

If during a call to [^DDS](#dds-invoke-screenman) you get a hard error, you can DO RESET^DDS to:

- Reset the terminal characteristics.
- Unlock any locked records.
- Clean up some variables in the local symbol table.
- Remove the temporary data ScreenMan stores in ^TMP.

Since RESET^DDS does *not* clean up all local variables, you should DOP^DI afterwards to clean up any variables that RESET^DDS missed.

You can also use RESET^DDS if you get a hard error while using the Form Editor.

# ScreenMan Form Editor

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

## Introduction

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

The ScreenMan Form Editor is a screen-oriented tool for creating and editing ScreenMan forms. It allows you to select and drag form elements and edit their properties through a ScreenMan interface. It can run on character-based terminals (e.g., DEC VT-100 and Qume QVT-102), if properly defined through the Device Handler.

As you use the Form Editor, it is helpful to display or have printouts of the data dictionaries of the files containing the fields you are placing on ScreenMan forms. You need to know such things as the data dictionary numbers of files and subfiles and the maximum length of the external form of data.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/312.png) REF: See also:

- “<u>ScreenMan Forms</u>” section.
- “<u>ScreenMan API</u>” section, which describes the ScreenMan APIs you can use to load a form and to use from within a form.
- The *ScreenMan Tutorial for Developers Manual* located on the [VDL](http://www.va.gov/vdl/application.asp?appid=5).

## Invoking the Form Editor

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To invoke the Form Editor, perform the following steps from the VA FileMan menu \[DIUSER\]:

1.  At the “Select VA FileMan Option:” prompt, enter the Other Options \[DIOTHER\] option.
2.  At the “Select Other Options Option:” prompt, enter the ScreenMan \[DDS SCREEN MENU\] menu.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/313.png) NOTE: The ScreenMan \[DDS SCREEN MENU\] menu is locked with the XUSCREENMAN security key.

3.  At the “Select ScreenMan Option:” prompt enter the EDIT/CREATE A FORM \[DDS EDIT/CREATE A FORM\] option, as shown in <u>Figure 245</u>:

> <span id="_Ref494360378" class="anchor"></span>Figure 245: ScreenMan Form Editor—EDIT/CREATE A FORM Option: Invoking the Form Editor

Select VA FileMan Option: <span class="mark">OTHER OPTIONS</span>

Select Other Options Option: <span class="mark">SCREENMAN</span>

Select ScreenMan Option: <span class="mark">?</span>

Answer with SCREENMAN OPTION NUMBER, or NAME

Choose from:

1 EDIT/CREATE A FORM

2 RUN A FORM

3 DELETE A FORM

4 PURGE UNUSED BLOCKS

Select SCREENMAN OPTION: <span class="mark">EDIT/CREATE A FORM</span>

20. You are asked to select a file:

> <span id="_Toc204977031" class="anchor"></span>Figure 246: ScreenMan Form Editor—EDIT/CREATE A FORM Option: Selecting a File

EDIT/CREATE FORM FOR WHAT FILE:

21. And a form:

> <span id="_Toc204977032" class="anchor"></span>Figure 247: ScreenMan Form Editor—EDIT/CREATE A FORM Option: Selecting a Form

Select FORM:

At the “Select FORM:” prompt, you can either:

- Select an existing form to edit.
- Create a new form by entering a new form name.

If you create a new form (Figure 248), the Form Editor automatically creates one page on that form. The new page is given a:

- Page Number of 1.
- Page Name of “Page 1”.
- Page Coordinate of “1,1”.

> <span id="_Ref494368856" class="anchor"></span>Figure 248: ScreenMan Form Editor—EDIT/CREATE A FORM Option: Creating a New Form

*\<Enter Form Fields here\>*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

File: OPTION (#19) R1,C1

Form: ZZTEST (#86)

Page: 1 (Page 1)

\<PF1\>Q=Quit \<PF1\>E=Exit \<PF1\>S=Save \<PF1\>V=Block Viewer \<PF1\>H=Help

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/314.png) NOTE: You can also use the Programmer mode utility [^DDGF](#ddgf-invoke-the-form-editor-from-programmer-mode) to invoke the Form Editor.

## Command Summary

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

This section describes the following Form Editor functionality:

- <u>Navigating on the Main Screen and Block Viewer Screen</u>
- <u>Quick Page Navigation</u>
- <u>Moving Screen Elements</u>
- <u>Adding, Selecting, and Editing</u>

### Navigating on the Main Screen and Block Viewer Screen

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

| To Move the Cursor        | Press                      |
|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Up one line               | \<ARROW UP\>           |
| Down one line             | \<ARROW DOWN\>         |
| Right one column          | \<ARROW RIGHT\>        |
| Left one column           | \<ARROW LEFT\>         |
| One field to the right    | \<Tab\>                |
| One field to the left     | Q                      |
| Five columns to the right | S                      |
| Five columns to the left  | A                      |
| Top of screen             | \<PF1\>\<ARROW UP\>    |
| Bottom of screen          | \<PF1\>\<ARROW DOWN\>  |
| Right edge of screen      | \<PF1\>\<ARROW RIGHT\> |
| Left edge of screen       | \<PF1\>\<ARROW LEFT\>  |

<span id="_Ref389651840" class="anchor"></span>Table 95: ScreenMan Form Editor—Shortcuts at the CAPTION Prompt

To switch between the Main screen and the Block Viewer screen:

Press \<PF1\>V.

### Quick Page Navigation

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

| To                                        | Press                                                                         |
|-------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Go to the next page                       | \<PF1\>\<PF1\>\<ARROW DOWN\>                                              |
| Go to the previous page                   | \<PF1\>\<PF1\>\<ARROW UP\>                                                |
| Go into a subpage associated with a field | Select the field with Spacebar *or*Enter keys and press \<PF1\>D |

<span id="_Ref494451759" class="anchor"></span>Table 96: ^DDS API—Error Codes Returned

To select a screen element (field caption, field data, or block name):

1.  Position the cursor over the element.
2.  Press Spacebar *or*Enter.
3.  Press Spacebar *or*Enter again to deselect the element.

To reorder all fields on a block:

1.  Select the block on the Block Viewer Screen.
2.  Press \<PF1\>O.

### Moving Screen Elements

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

| To Drag a Selected Element | Press                      |
|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| Up one line                | \<ARROW UP\>           |
| Down one line              | \<ARROW DOWN\>         |
| Right one column           | \<ARROW RIGHT\>        |
| Left one column            | \<ARROW LEFT\>         |
| Five columns to the right  | \<Tab\> or S       |
| Five columns to the left   | Q or A             |
| Top of screen              | \<PF1\>\<ARROW UP\>    |
| Bottom of screen           | \<PF1\>\<ARROW DOWN\>  |
| Right edge of screen       | \<PF1\>\<ARROW RIGHT\> |
| Left edge of screen        | \<PF1\>\<ARROW LEFT\>  |

<span id="_Ref494726988" class="anchor"></span>Table 97: BROWSE^DDBR API—Error Codes Returned

### Adding, Selecting, and Editing

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

| To                              | Press                        |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Select or create a new form     | \<PF1\>M or \<PF2\>M |
| Select another page             | \<PF1\>P                 |
| Add a new page                  | \<PF2\>P                 |
| Add a new block                 | \<PF2\>B                 |
| Add a new field                 | \<PF2\>F                 |
| Edit properties of current form | \<PF4\>M                 |
| Edit properties of current page | \<PF4\>P                 |

<span id="_Ref494729039" class="anchor"></span>Table 98: WP^DDBR API—Error Codes Returned

To invoke the ScreenMan form to edit field or block properties:

1.  Select the field or block.
2.  Press PF4.

To edit the caption of a field on the Main Screen:

1.  Position the cursor over the caption.
2.  Press PF3.
22. Press the Enter key when finished editing.

To edit the data length of a field on the Main Screen:

1.  Position the cursor over the underline that represents the data.
2.  Press PF3.
23. Press \<ARROWRIGHT\> and \<ARROWLEFT\> to change the length.
24. Press the Enter key when finished.

## Form Editor’s Main Screen

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

<u>Figure 249</u> is an example of the Form Editor’s Main Screen.

The top portion of the Main Screen is the Work Area. Here you see field captions, as well as underscores representing data fields, for fields that are defined on the blocks of the current page. Each of these items is called a screen element. This area of the screen is the one that you control when you display information to the user on a form.

<span id="_Ref388970309" class="anchor"></span>Figure 249: ScreenMan Form Editor—Main Screen

NAME: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

STREET ADDRESS: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

CITY: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

STATE: \_\_

ZIP CODE: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

File: ZZFILE NAME (# nnnn) Rn,Cn

Form: ZZFORM NAME

Page: n (ZZName of Page)

\<PF1\>Q=Quit \<PF1\>E=Exit \<PF1\>S=Save \<PF1\>V=Block Viewer \<PF1\>H=Help

The bottom portion of the screen contains status information, such as:

- Name and number of the file to which the form is attached.
- Name of the form.
- Number and name of the page you are currently editing.
- “Rn,Cn” at the lower right of the screen indicates the current row and column position of the cursor.

When a user runs a form, this portion of the screen is occupied by ScreenMan’s command area.

### Exiting, Quitting, Saving, and Obtaining Help

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

You can exit from the Form Editor’s Main Screen in one of two ways:

- Press \<PF1\>E to exit and save any changes you made to the following:
- Field captions
- Data lengths of fields
- Block names
- Page, block, and field coordinates.

These are the properties that are visible on the Form Editor screens.

- Press \<PF1\>Q to quit and discard the changes you made to those properties.

You can also save changes without leaving the Form Editor by pressing \<PF1\>S.

Pressing \<PF1\>H accesses the Form Editor’s online help screens.

| To                          | Press        |
|-----------------------------|--------------|
| Exit and save changes       | \<PF1\>E |
| Quit without saving changes | \<PF1\>Q |
| Save without exiting        | \<PF1\>S |
| Bring up help screens       | \<PF1\>H |
| Move to Block Viewer screen | \<PF1\>V |

<span id="_Ref494732919" class="anchor"></span>Table 99: DOCLIST^DDBR API—Error Codes Returned

## Block Viewer Screen

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To view the blocks on the current page, press \<PF1\>V to go to the Block Viewer Screen. The Block Viewer Screen displays the names of the blocks defined on the current page. For example, if the current page contains blocks called ZZBLOCK NAME 1 and ZZBLOCK NAME 2, the Block Viewer Screen looks like <u>Figure 250</u>:

<span id="_Ref494375656" class="anchor"></span>Figure 250: ScreenMan Form Editor—Block Viewer Screen

ZZBLOCK NAME 1

ZZBLOCK NAME 2

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

File: ZZFILE NAME (# nnnn) BLOCK VIEWER R1,C1

Form: ZZFORM NAME

Page: n (ZZPage Name)

\<PF1\>V=Main Screen \<PF1\>H=Help

Like the captions and data fields displayed on the [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen), the block names on the Block Viewer Screen are screen elements.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/315.png) NOTE: The words “BLOCK VIEWER” appear in the bottom portion of the Block Viewer Screen.

To return to the Main Screen:

Press \<PF1\>V.

## Navigating on the Form Editor Screens

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To move the cursor on the [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen) and the [Block Viewer Screen](#block-viewer-screen), you can use the key sequences listed in <u>Table 92</u>.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/316.png) NOTE: You can move the cursor only within the boundaries of the current page, as determined by the page coordinate.

| To Move the Cursor         | Press                      |
|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| Up one line.               | \<ARROW UP\>           |
| Down one line.             | \<ARROW DOWN\>         |
| Right one column.          | \<ARROW RIGHT\>        |
| Left one column.           | \<ARROW LEFT\>         |
| One field to the right.    | \<Tab\> or S       |
| One field to the left.     | Q or A             |
| Five columns to the right. | S                      |
| Five columns to the left.  | A                      |
| Top of screen.             | \<PF1\>\<ARROW UP\>    |
| Bottom of screen.          | \<PF1\>\<ARROW DOWN\>  |
| Right edge of screen.      | \<PF1\>\<ARROW RIGHT\> |
| Left edge of screen.       | \<PF1\>\<ARROW LEFT\>  |

<span id="_Ref494790459" class="anchor"></span>Table 100: \$\$UTC^DIUTC API—Error Messages Returned

## Going to Another Page

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

In the Form Editor, you work with one page at a time. The page with which you are currently working is indicated in the status area at the bottom portion of the screen. To go to another page, press \<PF1\>P. The Form Editor asks you to select another page on the form:

<span id="_Ref87275869" class="anchor"></span>Figure 251: ScreenMan Form Editor—Going to Another Page

Select PAGE: *<span class="mark">n</span>*

At the “Select PAGE:” prompt (<u>Figure 251</u>) you can enter ? (a single question mark) to get a list of the pages defined on the form. The page you select becomes the current page and the Form Editor displays the fields on that page in the Work Area of the [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen).

Shortcut keys listed in <u>Table 93</u> also allow you to quickly change the current page:

| Shortcut Key Sequence           | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
|---------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| \<PF1\>\<PF1\>\<ARROWDOWN\> | If the current page has a [Next Page](#screenman_next_page_property) defined, you can press this key sequenced to go to the next page.                                                                                              |
| \<PF1\>\<PF1\>\<ARROWUP\>   | If the current page has a [Previous Page](#screenman_previous_page_property) defined, you can press this key sequence to go to the previous page.                                                                                   |
| \<PF1\>D                    | If one of the fields on the current page has a subpage associated with it, you can go to that subpage by first selecting the field (press Spacebar *or*Enter over the caption of that field) and then press this key sequence. |
| \<PF1\>C                    | To close a subpage and return to the page underneath, press this key sequence.                                                                                                                                                          |

<span id="_Ref494967453" class="anchor"></span>Table 101: FILE^DDMP API—Error Codes Returned

## Adding Pages, Blocks, and Fields

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

- <u>Adding Pages (\<PF2\>P)</u>
- <u>Adding Blocks \<PF2\>B</u>
- <u>Adding Fields \<PF2\>F</u>

### Adding Pages (\<PF2\>P)

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To add a new page to the form, press \<PF2\>P. The Form Editor asks you to enter the page number of the new page:

<span id="_Toc204977037" class="anchor"></span>Figure 252: ScreenMan Form Editor—Adding a Page

NEW PAGE NUMBER:

Here you *must* enter a page number that has *not* yet been used on the form. Press \<PF1\>Q to close this “popup” page and abort adding a new page. Once you have selected a new page number, the Form Editor asks:

<span id="_Toc204977038" class="anchor"></span>Figure 253: ScreenMan Form Editor—Adding a Page Confirmation

Are you adding Page *n*

as a new page on this form?

If you answer YES, the Form Editor invokes a ScreenMan form in which you can edit the properties of the new page.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/317.png) REF: See the “<u>Editing Page Properties</u>” section.

### Adding Blocks \<PF2\>B

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To add a new block to the current page, move the cursor to the location on the page where you want the upper left corner of the block positioned, and press \<PF2\>B. The Form Editor asks you for the name of the block you want to add to the current page:

<span id="_Toc204977039" class="anchor"></span>Figure 254: ScreenMan Form Editor—Adding a Block

Select NEW BLOCK NAME:

Here, you can either select an existing block from the BLOCK (#.404) file or enter the name of a new block. If you enter the name of a new block (e.g., ZZTEST BLOCK 1), the Form Editor asks you whether you wish to add the block to the BLOCK (#.404) file:

<span id="_Toc204977040" class="anchor"></span>Figure 255: ScreenMan Form Editor—Adding a Block Confirmation

ARE YOU ADDING 'ZZTEST BLOCK 1' AS A NEW BLOCK (THE 36TH)?

And, whether you want to add the block to the current page of the form:

<span id="_Toc204977041" class="anchor"></span>Figure 256: ScreenMan Form Editor—Adding a Block to a Page

Are you adding ZZTEST BLOCK 1 as a new block on this page?

If you answer YES to these questions, the Form Editor invokes a ScreenMan form where you can edit the properties of the new block.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/318.png) REF: See the “<u>Editing Block Properties</u>” section.

#### Header Blocks

For backward compatibility, the Form Editor displays and allows you to edit the properties of Header Blocks already defined on the form. It does *not*, however, provide a way to add Header Blocks to a form, since Display-type blocks provide the same functionality as Header Blocks. Instead of creating a Header Block on a page, you can create a Display-type block with a coordinate of “1,1” relative to the page.

### Adding Fields \<PF2\>F

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To add fields to a block on the current page of the form, you *must* be on the Form Editor’s [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen). If you are currently on the [Block Viewer Screen](#block-viewer-screen), press \<PF1\>V to return to the Form Editor [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen). Before you can add fields, at least one block *must* be defined on the current page.

To add a field, move the cursor to the desired location of the new field and press \<PF2\>F. The Form Editor presents the dialog in <u>Figure 257</u>:

<span id="_Ref389650269" class="anchor"></span>Figure 257: ScreenMan Form Editor—Adding Fields

Select BLOCK:

FIELD ORDER:

FIELD TYPE:

To close this “popup” page and abort adding a new field, press \<PF1\>Q.

You can change any of the default answers the Form Editor provides. The Form Editor asks you to select a block on which to add the new field. You can select only those blocks that are defined on the current page. The Form Editor also asks you for the [Field Order](#screenman_field_order_property) number and the [Field Type](#screenman_field_type_property) of the new field.

Once you have filled in all the information in this “popup” page, press \<PF1\>E. The Form Editor adds the new field to the block and invokes a form where you can edit the properties of the field just created.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/319.png) REF: See the “<u>Editing Field Properties</u>” section.

## Selecting and Moving Screen Elements

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

### Selecting Screen Elements

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

The items you see on the Form Editor’s [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen) and [Block Viewer Screen](#block-viewer-screen) are called screen elements. They include field captions and data fields shown on the [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen), and block names shown on the [Block Viewer Screen](#block-viewer-screen):

- To select a screen element, press Spacebar or Enter over the element.
- To deselect an element, press Spacebar or Enter again.

### Moving Screen Elements

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To move a screen element, such as a field or block to a new location:

1.  Position the cursor over the element.
2.  Select it with Spacebar *or*Enter keys.
3.  Use the key sequences in <u>Table 94</u> to move the element.

| To Drag an Element        | Press this Key Sequence    |
|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Up one line               | \<ARROW UP\>           |
| Down one line             | \<ARROW DOWN\>         |
| Right one column          | \<ARROW RIGHT\>        |
| Left one column           | \<ARROW LEFT\>         |
| Five columns to the right | \<Tab\> or S       |
| Five columns to the left  | Q or A             |
| Top of screen             | \<PF1\>\<ARROW UP\>    |
| Bottom of screen          | \<PF1\>\<ARROW DOWN\>  |
| Right edge of screen      | \<PF1\>\<ARROW RIGHT\> |
| Left edge of screen       | \<PF1\>\<ARROW LEFT\>  |

<span id="_Ref389652616" class="anchor"></span>Table 102: EN^DIAXU—Error Codes Returned

You can drag a field only within the boundaries of the block on which it is defined, and you can drag a block only within the boundaries of the page on which it is defined.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/320.png) NOTE: No matter where you move a field, it remains associated with the block on which it was originally defined.

If you select the caption of a field, both the caption and data portion of the field, if one exists, are selected and can be dragged as a single unit. If you select the data portion of a field, only the data portion is selected and can be dragged independently of the caption.

If you drag a block name to a new location on the [Block Viewer Screen](#block-viewer-screen), all fields on that block move to a new location.

The [Block Coordinate](#screenman_block_coordinate_property) of a block defines the upper left boundary of the block. The block boundary extends from that coordinate to the lower right edge of the Display/Edit Area.

Similarly, the [Page Coordinate](#screenman_page_coordinate_property) of a page defines the upper left boundary of the page. If the page is a regular page, the page boundary extends from that coordinate to the lower right edge of the Display/Edit Area. If the page is a “popup” page, the [Lower Right Coordinate](#screenman_lower_righ_coordinate_property) of the page defines the lower right boundary of the page.

## Editing Properties

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

### Editing Field Properties

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To edit the properties of a field:

1.  Select the field with Spacebar *or*Enter keys.
2.  Press PF4.
3.  The Form Editor invokes a ScreenMan form where the properties of the field can be edited.

The specific form that is invoked depends on the type of the selected field. For example, the form for editing DATA DICTIONARY fields looks like <u>Figure 258</u>:

<span id="_Ref458179598" class="anchor"></span>Figure 258: ScreenMan Form Editor—Editing Field Properties

----------------------- Data Dictionary Field Properties -----------------------

\| \|

\| FIELD ORDER: FIELD: \|

\| ORDER PARAMETERS... SUPPRESS COLON AFTER CAPTION? \|

\| UNIQUE NAME: \|

\| \|

\| CAPTION: \|

\| DEFAULT: \|

\| EXECUTABLE CAPTION: \|

\| EXECUTABLE DEFAULT: \|

\| \|

\| BRANCHING LOGIC: \|

\| PRE ACTION: \|

\| POST ACTION: \|

\| POST ACTION ON CHANGE: \|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When you enter a value at the “FIELD:” prompt for DATA DICTIONARY fields, the Form Editor automatically defines the Caption as the field’s label:

- Multiple field—If the field is a Multiple field, the Form Editor adds the word “Select” before the field’s label.
- WORD-PROCESSING field—If the field is a WORD-PROCESSING field, the Form Editor adds the characters “(WP)” after the field’s label.

At the “CAPTION:” prompt, you can:

- Accept the Form Editor’s default.
- Enter a new caption.
- Enter one of the captions listed in <u>Table 95</u>:

<table>
<caption><p><span id="_Ref389652674" class="anchor"></span>Table 103: EXTRACT^DIAXU API—Error Codes Returned</p></caption>
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 37%" />
<col style="width: 62%" />
</colgroup>
<thead>
<tr class="header">
<th>To Define the Caption as</th>
<th>Enter at the CAPTION Prompt</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr class="odd">
<td>Field label</td>
<td><strong>!L</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td>Field title</td>
<td><strong>!T</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr class="odd">
<td>Unique name of field</td>
<td><strong>!U</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr class="even">
<td>Duplicated string</td>
<td><strong>!DUP(string,number of occurrences)</strong><br />
For example, <strong>!DUP(“-”,79)</strong></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<span id="_Ref389652674" class="anchor"></span>Table 103: EXTRACT^DIAXU API—Error Codes Returned

The “OTHER PARAMETERS:” prompt is followed by an ellipsis (...) to indicate that this field leads to a new page. To view that page:

1.  Navigate to the Other Parameters field.
2.  Press the Enter key.
3.  A “popup” window appears where you can edit additional properties of the field.

<span id="_Ref322691234" class="anchor"></span>Figure 259: ScreenMan Form Editor—Other Parameters

----------------------- Data Dictionary Field Properties -----------------------

\| \|

\| ---------------------------- Other Parameters ----------------------------- \|

\| \| \| \|

\| \| REQUIRED: \_\_\_ DISPLAY GROUP: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ \| \|

\| \| DISABLE EDITING: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ RIGHT JUSTIFY: \_\_\_\_ \| \|

\| \| SUB PAGE LINK: \_\_\_\_\_\_ \| \|

\| \| \| \|

\| \| DATA LENGTH: \_\_\_ \| \|

\| \| CAPTION COORDINATE: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ \| \|

\| \| DATA COORDINATE: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ \| \|

\| \| \| \|

\| \| DATA VALIDATION: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ \| \|

\| \| \| \|

\| --------------------------------------------------------------------------- \|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To close the Other Parameters “popup” window, press \<PF1\>C. To return to the Form Editor’s [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen), press \<PF1\>E to exit and save your changes, or press \<PF1\>Q to quit the form without saving your changes.

#### Editing Field Captions and Data Length

As described in the “<u>Editing Field Properties</u>” section, you can press PF4 to invoke a ScreenMan form to edit the [Caption](#screenman_caption_property) and [Data Length](#screenman_data_length_property) of fields. You can also edit these properties directly from the Form Editor’s [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen).

To change the Caption of a field:

1.  Position the cursor over the caption.
25. Press PF3.
26. Edit the caption with the same editing keys available in ScreenMan’s Field Editor.
27. Press Enter when you have finished editing the caption.

To change the Data Length of a field, do the following:

1.  Position the cursor over the data portion of the field.
2.  Press PF3.
3.  Increase and decrease the Data Length by pressing \<ARROW RIGHT\> and \<ARROW LEFT\>. An indicator (L=n) at the lower right portion of the [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen) indicates the current Data Length.
4.  Press the Enter key when you have finished editing the Data Length.

#### Reordering All Fields on a Block

After creating and arranging all the fields on a block, you can quickly make the [Field Order](#screenman_field_order_property) of all the fields equivalent to the tab order by doing the following:

1.  Go to the [Block Viewer Screen](#block-viewer-screen) (\<PF1\>V).
2.  Select the block (Spacebar *or*Enter over the block name).
3.  Press \<PF1\>O.

Remember that the Field Order is the order in which the elements on the block are traversed when the user presses Enter. The \<PF1\>O key sequence reassigns Field Order numbers to all the elements on the block, so that the Enter key takes the user from field to field in the same order as the Tab key (left to right, top to bottom).

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/321.png) NOTE: If you refer to fields by [Field Order](#screenman_field_order_property) in places, such as [Branching Logic](#screenman_branching_logic_property), [Pre Action](#screenman_pre_action_field_property), and [Post Action](#screenman_post_action_field_property), reordering the fields on the block could cause that code to refer to the wrong fields. You *must* then modify the code to either reflect the new Field Order numbers or refer to those fields by [Caption](#screenman_caption_property) or [Unique Name](#screenman_unique_name_property) instead.

### Editing Block Properties

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To edit the properties of a block on the current page:

1.  Press \<PF1\>V to go to the [Block Viewer Screen](#block-viewer-screen).
2.  Select the block name with Spacebar *or*Enter keys.
3.  Press PF4.
4.  The Form Editor invokes a ScreenMan form where the properties of the block can be edited.

The form for editing block properties looks like <u>Figure 260</u>:

<span id="_Ref458179663" class="anchor"></span>Figure 260: ScreenMan Form Editor—Editing Block Properties

--------------------- Block Properties Stored in FORM File ---------------------

\| \|

\| BLOCK NAME: BLOCK ORDER: \|

\| TYPE OF BLOCK: OTHER PARAMETERS... \|

\| POINTER LINK: \|

\| \|

\| PRE ACTION: \|

\| POST ACTION: \|

\| \|

\|--------------------- Block Properties Stored in BLOCK File --------------------\|

\| \|

\| NAME: DESCRIPTION (WP): \|

\| DD NUMBER: DISABLE NAVIGATION: \|

\| \|

\| PRE ACTION: \|

\| POST ACTION: \|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The fields on the top portion of the preceding screen are fields from the FORM (#.403) file. Changes to the values of the fields in this area affect the block only as it is used on this form.

The fields on the bottom portion of the screen are fields from the BLOCK (#.404) file. Changes to the values of the fields in this area affect the properties of the block itself, and thus, affect any form that uses this block.

When you create a new block, make sure that the [DD Number](#screenman_dd_number_property) is correct. The Form Editor provides a default DD Number equal to the Primary file of the form. If you are creating a block that contains fields from a subfile, or from a file to which you are navigating, you *must* change the DD Number.

### Editing Page Properties

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/322.png) REF: For information on how to move from page to page when editing a form, see the “<u>Going to Another Page</u>” section.

To edit the properties of the current page, press \<PF4\>P from the Form Editor’s [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen). The form for editing page properties looks like <u>Figure 261</u>:

<span id="_Ref458179698" class="anchor"></span>Figure 261: ScreenMan Form Editor—Editing Page Properties

------------------------------- Page Properties --------------------------------

\| \|

\| PAGE NUMBER: \|

\| PAGE NAME: \|

\| HEADER BLOCK: \|

\| \|

\| PAGE COORDINATE: IS THIS A POP UP PAGE? \|

\| LOWER RIGHT COORDINATE: \|

\| NEXT PAGE: \|

\| PREVIOUS PAGE: \|

\| PARENT FIELD: \|

\| \|

\| DESCRIPTION (WP): \|

\| PRE ACTION: \|

\| POST ACTION: \|

\| \|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you want the page to be a “popup” page (window):

1.  At the “IS THIS A POP UP PAGE?” prompt, enter YES.
2.  Enter a value for “LOWER RIGHT COORDINATE”.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/323.png) NOTE: VA FileMan 22.2 supports screens longer than 24 lines.

#### Editing “Popup” Page Coordinates

As described in Section <u>5.10.3</u>, “<u>Editing Page Properties</u>,”, you can press \<PF4\>P to invoke a ScreenMan form to edit the properties of the current page. You can also change the coordinate of a “popup” page directly from the Form Editor’s [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen), by selecting and dragging the border of the “popup” page.

<u>Figure 262</u> is an example of the Main Screen of the Form Editor where the current page is a “popup” page.

<span id="_Ref342294043" class="anchor"></span>Figure 262: ScreenMan Form Editor—Editing “Popup” Page Coordinates

THIS IS A CAPTION ONLY FIELD

-------------------------------------------------

NAME: \_\_\_\_ \_\_\_\| \|

SSN: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\| This is the pop-up page \|

SELECT PHONE: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\| \|

\| \|

\| \|

-------------------------------------------------

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

File: ZZFILE NAME (# nnnn) R1,C1

Form: ZZFORM NAME

Page: n (ZZName of Pop-Up Page)

\<PF1\>Q=Quit \<PF1\>E=Exit \<PF1\>S=Save \<PF1\>V=Block Viewer \<PF1\>H=Help

To move the entire “popup” page around on the [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen), do the following:

1.  Position the cursor anywhere on the top boundary of the “popup” page.
28. Press Spacebar *or*Enter to select it.
29. Use the navigational keys described in the “<u>Moving Screen Elements</u>” section to drag the entire “popup” page to a new location.
30. Press Spacebar *or*Enter again to lock the page in its new position.

To resize the “popup” page (i.e., to change the lower right coordinate of the page), do the following:

1.  Position the cursor over the lower right corner of the page boundary.
31. Press Spacebar *or*Enter to select it.
32. Use the navigational keys to move the corner to a new location.
33. Press Spacebar *or*Enter again when the page is the correct size.

### Editing Form Properties

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To edit the properties of the form, press \<PF4\>M from the Form Editor’s [Main Screen](#form-editors-main-screen). The form for editing form properties looks like <u>Figure 263</u>:

<span id="_Ref494445182" class="anchor"></span>Figure 263: ScreenMan Form Editor—Editing Form Properties

------------------------------- Form Properties --------------------------------

\| \|

\| NAME: \|

\| TITLE: \|

\| \|

\| PRE ACTION: \|

\| POST ACTION: \|

\| DATA VALIDATION: \|

\| POST SAVE: \|

\| \|

\| DESCRIPTION: RECORD SELECTION PAGE: \|

\| \|

\| \|

\| READ ACCESS: \|

\| WRITE ACCESS: \|

\| \|

\| \|

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Choosing Another Form

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

You can select another form to edit or create a new form without leaving the Form Editor. Press \<PF1\>M or \<PF2\>M to select another file and form. You see the same prompts described in the section “Invoking the Form Editor”:

<span id="_Toc204977049" class="anchor"></span>Figure 264: ScreenMan Form Editor—Choosing Another Form

EDIT/CREATE FORM FOR WHAT FILE:

And:

<span id="_Toc204977050" class="anchor"></span>Figure 265: ScreenMan Form Editor—Select Form

Select FORM:

If you select a different form or create a new form, and changes to the previous form (e.g., ZZTEST) have *not* yet been saved, the Form Editor asks:

<span id="_Toc204977051" class="anchor"></span>Figure 266: ScreenMan Form Editor—Save Changes

Save changes to form ZZTEST? YES//

This gives you the opportunity to save or discard your changes before moving on to the next form.

## Deleting Screen Elements (Fields, Blocks, Pages, and Forms)

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

In general, to delete a screen element:

1.  Select and edit the properties of the element.
2.  Enter an at-sign (@) at the first field of the ScreenMan form.

### Deleting Fields

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To delete a field, select the field by pressing Spacebar *or*Enter over the caption of the field, press PF4 to invoke the form to edit the properties of the field, and then enter an at-sign (@) at the “FIELD ORDER:” prompt.

### Deleting Blocks

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

Similarly, to delete a block:

1.  Select the block on the [Block Viewer Screen](#block-viewer-screen).
2.  Press PF4 to invoke the form to edit block properties.
3.  Enter an at-sign (@) at the “BLOCK NAME:” prompt.
4.  Answer YES to the warning that deletions are done immediately.
5.  If the block is *not* used on any other forms, the Form Editor also asks whether you want to delete the block from the BLOCK (#.404) file.  
      
    If you choose *not* to delete the block from the BLOCK (#.404) file, you can subsequently delete the block only by running the ScreenMan Purge Unused Blocks \[DDS PURGE UNUSED BLOCKS\] option.

### Deleting Pages

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

To delete a page:

1.  Make that page the current page.
2.  Press \<PF4\>P to invoke the form to edit page properties.
3.  Enter an at-sign (@) at the “PAGE NUMBER:” prompt.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/324.png) REF: For information on how to move from page to page when editing a form, see the “<u>Going to Another Page</u>” section.

### Deleting Forms

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

You *cannot* delete a form from the Form Editor. To delete a form:

1.  Exit the Form Editor.
34. Perform the steps in <u>Figure 267</u> from the VA FileMan \[DIUSER\] menu:

<span id="_Ref389652403" class="anchor"></span>Figure 267: ScreenMan Form Editor—Choosing Another Form

Select VA FileMan Option: <span class="mark">OTHER OPTIONS</span>

Select Other Options Option: <span class="mark">SCREENMAN</span>

Select ScreenMan Option: <span class="mark">?</span>

Answer with SCREENMAN OPTION NUMBER, or NAME

Choose from:

1 EDIT/CREATE A FORM

2 RUN A FORM

3 DELETE A FORM

4 PURGE UNUSED BLOCKS

Select SCREENMAN OPTION: <span class="mark">D \<Enter\></span> ELETE A FORM

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/325.png) REF: For more information on this menu option, see the “<u>ScreenMan Menu Options</u>” section in the “<u>ScreenMan Forms</u>” section.

# ScreenMan API

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

## Introduction

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

VA FileMan’s ScreenMan utility provides a screen-oriented interface for editing and displaying data. The APIs described in this section provide entry points for loading a ScreenMan form and entry points you can use at various places within a ScreenMan form.

![](fm-22-2-developer-s-guide/326.png) REF: See also:

- The “<u>ScreenMan Forms</u>” section.
- The “<u>ScreenMan Form Editor</u>” section.
- The *ScreenMan Tutorial for Developers Manual* located on the [VDL](http://www.va.gov/vdl/application.asp?appid=5).

## ^DDS: Invoke ScreenMan

<!-- back-to-toc -->[↑ Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

Reference Type

Supported

Category

ScreenMan

ICR#

10031

Description
